Threat:
- Malicious manifests introduced as if they came from trusted CI/CD pipeline
- Malicious service definitions impersonate legitimate EMS/PDC/SCADA-support services
- Bypass of CI/CD control by submitting unauthorized manifests directly to the Kubernetes API
Impact:
- the manifest is still trusted, so the system is compromised in terms of scaling and configuration of the cluster 
- malicious work appears as legitimate EMS/PDC services
- fake services intercept or manipulate grid processing pipelines
Mitigation:
- enforce all deployments with a single source of truth such as GitOps controllers
- sign all manifests and enforce signature verification at admission time
- use of cloud-native workload identity an bind ServiceAccounts to approved workloads strictly
- strong Kubernetes admission controllers to reject unsigned manifests and unknown service identities
- secure CI/CD authentication

Threat:
- Modify container images, change startup arguments, inject backdoors, change number of replicas
- Modify security parameters such as capabilities restrictions, seccomp or RBAC 
- Injection of ConfigMaps or altered configuration parameters
Impact:
- EMS and PDC could process altered grid data streams
- Analytics output unreliable
- Weaked security controls inside the cluser leading to an increased possibility of attack
- Communication paths could be slightly altered
Mitigation:
- using digests instead of tags
- enforce usage of immutable deployments where possible
- OPA rules
- Network policy enforcement with default deny policies and explicit allow lists
- Pod security standards (PSS) to enforce restricted profile, avoid privilege escalation and read-only root filesystem

Threat:
- Lack of traceability of manifest changes
- Missing audit trails, absense of commits or overwritement of deployment
- Shared identity deployment, when multiple actors use the same service account or CI identity
- Missing logs correlation between actions and identities
Impact:
-Not possible to determine who deployed or modified workload
- No linking between user and misbehavior
- forensics investigation difficult to perform
- critical infrastructure auditability requirements not realiable, leading to regulatory non compliance
Mitigation:
- immutable audit logging and log storage with WORM* 
- signed workflows with all changes committed in Git, signed and reviewed
- no shared identities or admin accounts
- timestamp integrity assurance
- maintain a link between the commits and the cluster changes

Threat:
- Exposure of cluster topology
- Exposure of internal endpoints and services
- Secrets leakage
- Exposure of configurations and security policies
- Operational workflow leakage with processing pipelines, analytics flow and system dependencies
Impact:
- Attacker will have a precise idea of how the system works and how it is organized, being able to build an accurate model of grid support architecture
- Facilitiating lateral movements and increasing the probability of successful compromisation of the operational system
Mitigation:
- strict RBAC policy per namespace with separation of operational ans non-operational clusters
- external secrets management
- avoid exposing internal structure
- encryption of data in transit and authentication of the entities sending and receiving data
- avoid using sensitive labels or sensitive label sanitization

Threat:
- Attacker uses stolen Kubernetes secrets to authenticate as legitimate services
- An attacker introduces malicious Secrets into the cluster that are accepted as valid credentials by workloads
- Stolen service account tokens are reused to authenticate as legitimate workloads
- Secrets from non-production environments are used to impersonate production services because of the weak separation
Impact:
- Malicious services appear as trusted EMS/PDC components
- Unauthorized workloads integrate into grid data pipelines
- Attackers gain access to operational communication paths
- Lateral movement across SCADA-support and analytics systems
- Loss of trust in workload identity model across clusters
Mitigation:
- workload identity management instead of static secrets
- short lived automatically rotated credentials
- mTLS for service communication
- identity binding with certidicate, not static secrets
- admission control validation, reject workloads using unapproved secrets, OPA

Threat:
- modification of secrets or replacement of legitimate ones with attacker controlled ones
- secret replacement attacks
- etcd secret store manipulation
- corruption of backups or replica secrets for persistent attacks
Impact:
- persistent compomise of the critical services
- inability to prove who accessed, some access could also remain undetected for a log time period
- pass the control of critical information to unlegitimate users while blocking out legitimate ones
- difficulty in unserstanding legitimate and malicious credentials over time
Mitigation:
- encryption of secrets at rest, use of HSM key management
- external secre managers
- avoid direct kubernets storage for high-critically credentials
- sign secret payload or metadata and verify its integrity before using them
- log all modification to secrets
- regular integrity checks

Threat:
- No traceability of who accessed Kubernetes Secrets, no audit trails or unreliable ones
- Multiple services or users use the same secret, preventing attribution
- No reliable record of secret state at a given point in time, in particular considering backup phases
- Compromisation of audit trails
Impact:
- Hard to take forensics analysis, so it is harder to solve smart grid issues and to understand whether the problem comes form an authorized or unauthorized user
- Delayed incident response for the loss or unreliability of logs
Mitigation:
- Immutable audit logs, encrypted at rest
- correspondance between secrets and workload, user identity, service account identity
- hash chaining technique for audit trails integrity

Threat:
- Unatuthorized access to etcd store
- Misconfigured RBAC secrets 
- Pod escape enabling secret extraction
- Dumping environmental variables
- Backup content exposure
- Secret data exposed through backups or cluster snapshots
Impact:
- Possibility to enter and have control of different parts of the system even if unauthorized 
- Lateral movements and persistent access
- Persistent unauthorized access to the infrastructure
Mitigation:
- encryption of data in tranist and at rest
- no direct usage of secrets but only reference
- least privilege principle
- secret isolation based on the domain to limit risks
- no secret logging
- encrypted + acccess control backups

Threat:
- Kubernetes admin credential impersonation obtained by different possible means e.g credential theft, malware, MFA bypass, session hijacking ecc.
- Control-plane component identity spoofing of kube-controller manager, kube-scheduler and kube-apiserver
- etcd certificate compromise
 - An attacker steals service account tokens used by SCADA, PDC, or supporting services and impersonates trusted workloads
Impact:
- the attacker can create or delete workloads on the cluster possibly dirsuprupting communication or control services needed controlling the smart grid
 - the attacker can modify secrets and RBAC policies, unauthorized deployment of compromised PDC or SCADA services considered legitimate and trusted
- impersonation of a legitimate controller using its credentials, malicious creation of resources, scaling and manipulation of deployments, increasing the possibility of a DDoS on the services deployed with this technology
- malicious etcd peer joins the cluster, partecipating in consensus mechanisms and disclosing cluster state and manipulating stored data, so increasing the possibility an attacker completing a successful attack
Mitigation:
- strong authenticatiion with MFA for admins and operators; hardware authentication mechanisms
- certificate rotation
- mutual authentication for all communication from and to the control plane (e.g. mTLS) 
- network separation, traffic filtering and monitoring mechanisms

Threat:
- etcd direct state manipulation
- modification of scheduling mechanisms or configuration
- modification of specific deployment configuration, such as container image, enviroment variables and startup variables
- manipulation of secrets stored in etcd database (e.g. PMU credentials, EMS database, service mesh certificates, ...)
- tampering of admission policies
Impact:
- alteration of configuration of containers, potential denail of service for PDC or SCADA control, giving the possibility to the attacker to act without being discovered 
- forcing scheduling of certain workload on specific nodes that could be compromised, increasing the attack surface or concentrating all the critical work on it
- execution of malicious code, manipulation of data to analyze producing incorrect results, possibility of data exfiltration. Reporting incorrect results and taking useless and misleading operational actions on the grid
- increased possibility of service impersonation and consequent disruption of trust chain
- security of the system is weakened without recognizing, anabling subdle attacks and access, this helps in other preparing more complex attacks to the grid
Mitigation:
- strict isolation of etcd and no direct admin access without controlled procedures
- version-controlled infrastructure 
- strong policy enforcement 
- signed container images and trusted registry only
- external secret management and detection of unauthorized changes

Threat:

 - Unattributable administrative actions, there is no possibility of determining who modified the deployment, chagned RBAC rules and accessed secret
- Service account withour traceability 
- Loss of correlation between: user identities, certificates, service accounts and administrative actions

Mitigation:
- Kubernetes logging 
- fine-grained API audit policies
- immutable logging and append-only storage
- named admin accounts only
-no shared admin identities
- certificate lifecycle tracking
- trusted time source and consisstent timestamps

Threat:
- secret extraction from etcd

- extraction of controller configuration

Impact:
- mass credential exposure, with potential critical consequence with the access to many different services and part of the system such as SCADA control service management or PDC manager

Threat:
- API server saturation
- etcd resource exhaustion due to excessive write operation on the system
- scheduler starvation by generating a huge number of pending pods
- many resource modification during operational work induces a long reconciliation time
Impact:
- control plane responsiveness is delayed, PDC and SCADA recovery could be upated or recovered with delay or incorrectly
- delay in consensus mechanisms, cluster state becomes unreliable and reconciliation requires an increasing amount of time and modification
-  increased time needed for scheduling, resulting in a delay of critical services such as SCADA low level control or PDC analysis
 - delay in the deployment of service and resources lost in useless modification
Mitigation:
- resource management with API rate limiting, resource quotas and admission controls                                                        - redundant etcd cluster 
- multi-node control plane (federation mechanism)
- performance monitoring
 etcd health monitoring
- API latency monitoring
- regular backup testing

- service account to cluster admin elevation of priviledge obtained by exploiting excessive permission vulnerabilities

- scheduler controlled by the attacker
-modification of RBAC policies and access configuration by modifying etcd stored configuration files

- persistent access to the system and governance over the services that this technology supports: SCADA control and PDC
Mitigation:
- RBAC policies with strong role separation with periodic reviews and least priviledge principle
- disabling of automatic token mounting
- MFA + PAM and dedicated admin environment                                               
- continuous monitoring for privilege escalation detection and RBAC changes                                                             
- restriction of privileged container and host namespaces access

Threat:

 - operational memory scraping for all workloads (SCADA contoller, PDC)

Impact:
- service impersonation, credentials are used to compromise other services

 - possibility to use reverse engineering to understand algorithms used in the smart grid, intellectual property theft

Threat:
- exhaustion of target machine resources in terms of CPU, memory or scheduling

- storage and volume exhaustion
- frenquent systems reboot of critical services leading to a loss of observability and control of the grid
Impact:
- processing latency of PDC and SCADA increases such that the attacker can have a larger impact without being discovered
 - the killed container service does not work until the system realises that it is missing, service is delayed also rebooting requires time. It can be part of a larger coordinated attack
- nodes are disconnected from the service, resilience is reduced
Mitigation:
- resource quotas, limits and priority classes

Threat:
- container escape 
- Kubelet credential abuse
- containers running in priviledge mode exploited to grant access on the host
- runtime vulnerability expolitation
Impact:
- permissions granted from the container to the host, that could impact all the services running on the same cluster (e.g. data processing and analysis in PDC)
 - workload manipulation resulting in algorithm compromisation, this could cause having too many false alarms or false positives
Mitigation:
- non-toot containers and minimal set of linux capabilities for every user
- runtime isolation with Seccomp, SELinux and/or AppArmor
- deny privileged mode by default
- hardened containerd configuration and secure kubelet configuration
- vulnerability management with image scanning, instant patching and node hardening
- runtime threat detection and privileded account monitoring

Impact:
- attackers impersonating high-level PDC can introduce false data and spread false workload to other high-level PDC or EMS

Mitigation:
- strong identity mangement with mechanisms such as mLTS , PKI and hardware protected keys
- federation trust controls with explcit whitelists, certificate pinning
- automated certificate rotation and HMS certidicate storage
- certificate anomaly detection and duplicate federation identities

Threat:

- PDC workload impersonation spoofing the credential of another workload
- IP spoofing used to bypass access control lists in ingress controller; host spoofing or HTTP header spoofing
- token of JWT token spoofing for API gateway
Impact:
- bypass of service level authorization, lateral movement and trusted service impersonation
- false PMU and PDC data injection leading to incorrect state estimation and potential incorrect operations
 - routing of malicious traffic to or attacker generated data to internal services
Mitigation:
- strong workload identity with unique identities for each service and hardware backed identities where possible
- mTLS between workloads, certificate validation and certificate pinning for critical services
- gateway security with strong authentication
- privileged access management and MFA

Asset: Service mesh and Network management services
Threat:
- modification of routing policies such that data streams do not reach their destination (e.g. PMU data not reaching PDC, PDC not reaching high-level PDC, ...) or reach incorrect ones or reaches malicious/undesired locations ( data exfiltration )
- authorization policies tampering
- rule about load balancing handling
Impact:
- loss of observability of the infected part of the smart grid, no information about its state and consequently on the actions to take to solve potential issues 
- internal system data sent to malicious entities  
- unlegitimate requests or data are authorized
- disrupton of load balancing service potentailly causing denial of service of the target part
- denial of service for traffic redirection
Mitigation:
- configuration integrity, signed configurations, configuration version control and multiple approval process
- automated policy validation and approval workflows
- continuous configuration monitoring and drift detection
- strict network policies ans segmentation

Asset: Service mesh and Network management services
Threat:
- logs about communication are compromised or deleted
- data or requests are done using common credentials or names 
- configurations, permissions and policies are modified without logging who did the changes and when 
- cetificate management repudiation, when an administator denies performing actions concerning certificates
Impact:
- accountability of changes is lost 
- accountability of who sends the data is compromised so that there is no guarantee that they are legitimate
- potential loss of compliance for data exfiltration
- malicious actions are not logged and not discovered rapidly, such that an attacker has more time to implemet malicious actions
- slower root-cause incidents research
Mitigation:
- immutable and centralized logging system with log retetion policies
- individual account only, in particular no shared admin accounts and unique workloads identity
- hash chaining an cryptographically signed logs
- protected timestamp source
- admin session recording, double approval workflows

Asset: Service mesh and Network management services
Threat:
- attacker access PDC traffic because of incorrect network policies 
- access to mesh configuration, topology and operational dependency
 - misconfigured gateway sending sentive data to malicious parties
- improper isolation between cloud environments exposes operational data belonging to different security domains
- API gateways or proxies reveal software versions, internal addresses, or infrastructure details useful for further attacks
Impact:
- malicious data redirection leading to attacker knowing the system topology and parameters
- intelligence exposure with configuration disclosure
- increased probability of successful attacks 
- regulatory compliance issues
Mitigation:
- encrytption of data in transit and at rest
- secure key management
- mutual authentication in communication
- zero-trust communications
- network segmentation of the different operational domains
- secure certificate storage
- sensitive field masking and data minimization

Asset: Service mesh and Network management services
Threat:
- certificate authority for service mesh not available
- API gateway is overwhelmed by an eccessive number of requests
- proxy service overload due to malformed requests
Impact:
- certificate expiration and impossibility for service accounts to prove their identity and use the system (PDC isolation, SCADA control isolation)
- API gateway does not respond properly to requests, communication of telemetry data is delayed decreasing the control and observability on the system 
- cloud architecture unreachable, resulting in delay in state estimation and analysis
Mitigation:
- high availability of mesh control plane, gateways and CA infrastructures
- resource control such as connection limits, rate limit and request quotas
- monitoring of endpoints and strong desaster recovery mechanisms
- retry limits and timeout controls

Asset: Service mesh and Network management services
Threat:
- compromisation of mesh service account 
- workload communicating directly without authorization, authentication, control
- comprimisation of CA 
- bypass of mesh security controls
- compromise of gateway management interfaces grants control over exposed services
Impact:
- control in routing, policy definition, security
- malicious workloads considered as legitimate could contain false data ecc.
- whole trust chain is compromised, unlegitimate service account could enter the system or reach admin priviledges
Mitigation:
- minimal RBAC permissions, separation of duties and dedicated administation roles
- token rotation and short-lived credentials
- offline CA root and intermediate hierarchy
- service mesh enforcement gateways
- privilege escalaition testing and policy compliance monitoring

Threat:
- developer impersonation ( credentials, VPN, MFA tokens, ecc.)
- impersonation of a legitimate CI worker
- depoyment controller spoofing
- signing service identity spoofing
Impact:
- by spoofing controls or credential of admin accounts, the attacker can introduce bugs, backdoors and vulnerabilities in the system, with the potential of compromising the system
- an attacker accessing the CI/CD repository can potentially disclosure the code, secrets and credentials.  this can dispatch intelligente about the logic and configuration of SCADA, PDC and communication services
- execution of malicious code
- bogus and malicious code is deployed because its origin appears to be the authorized system, malicious EMS, PDC control
- compromise signing procedures potentially deploying unreliable code
Mitigation:
- MFA for developers and administrators
- PAM for privileged users
- SSO and centralized IAM
- short-lived credentials
- mTLS between pipeline components
- workload identity mechanisms
- trusted package sources and repository pining
- signed commits and tags

Threat:
- change of the source repository to introduced backdoors, hidden functions or conditional logic
- compromisation of the script used for building the code
- poisoning of dependencies such as third party library which are know to be used
- container image tampering
- security tests, unit tests, or compliance checks are bypassed or falsified
Impact:
- injection of malicious code in the compilation if the code, while the soruce repository remains clear and the code in it is trusted. Deployment of malicious code with potential issues and compromised services
- attacker has methods to enter the system, read data, gain intelligence about the system and so perform better attacks
- pipleine imports automatically issues that can be exploited, compromisation of telemetry analysis, data exfiltration etc.
- block reboot and patching
Mitigation:
- software supply chain security and supply chain attestation
- signed commits, protected branches and mandatory reviews, multiple approval process
- singed image and image scanning
- immutable registries 
- approved repositories and dependency scanning
- immutable runners
- IaC security scanning

Threat:
- removal or modification of evidence linking software vulnerabilities to commits or users performing them 
- forgery of approval records
- artifact origin repudiation
- security test repudiation
Impact:
- loss of accountability of actions 
- loss  of correspondance between identity and credentials used
- harder to permanently block unlegitimate users or accounts
- loss of correspondance between attack or failure time and actions causing incidents
- harder to understand who changed the code in malicious version
- harder to detect problematic approval processes
Mitigation:
- immutable and centralized logging with SIEM integration
- no shared accounts
- session and approval recording
- signed commits, releases and build attestations
- mandatory reviewers
- separation of duties
- timestamp protection

Threat:
- running secrets are extracted and disclosed (e.g. credentials, configuration, deployment tokens)
- source code theft, exposure of algorithms, processing lofic and detection mechanims
- compromisation of signing key infrastucture
- diclosure of IaC repository
- build log and supply chain metadata disclosure
Impact:
- possibility to attack or infiltrate in other parts of the whole smart grid environment
- loss of proprietary data, technologies and strategies that could help attackers in elaborating successful attacks
- possibility to sign compromised code and make it look legitimate, execution of untrusted software compromising the base functionaliries of EMS, PDC and SCADA controls 
- exposure of cloud topology, communication paths, monitoring structure potentially leading to successful attacks thanks to the knowledge of the topology
- with compromised credentials, it is possible to have access to the code deployed, exfiltrate procedures and algorithms
Mitigation:
- short lived credentials, secret rotation, dynamic secrets and vault solutions
- encryption of configuration at rest and in transit 
- least privilege prinicple and role separation
- secret masking and logging

Threat:
- build infrastructure overload by flooding it with jobs
- unavailability of code storage platform
- GitOps synchronization failure
- deletion of CI/CD pipeline configuration
- logs, artifacts, or caches consume storage resources, causing pipeline failures
- loss of synchronization between desired and deployed states prevents updates or recovery procedures
Impact:
- delay in installation of security patches for vulnerable components, increasing the time the vulnerability can be exploited and used by an attacker
- dealy in reconfiguration of the system after attacks related to configuration changes or malicious code execution leaving the system in an unusable state or critical EMS and PDC functionalities altered to disrupt the desidered state analysis
Mitigation:
- redundant Git servers and replicated registries
- high availability deployment controllers
- resource controls such as job quotas, concurrency limits and rate limiting
- artifact caching and local mirroring of resources
- configuration backups
- queue monitoring, capacity monitoring and storage monitoring

Threat:
- developer gaining deployment credentials exploiting misconfigured pipeline permissions
- compromisation of signing service
Impact:
- unauthorized modification of PDC and SCADA service exploiting misconfigured pipeline permissions
 - compromised versions are not distinguishable from legitimate ones since they are all signed
Mitigation:
- fine frained RBAC
- separation of duties
- dedicated service accounts
- immutable build environment
- key rotation
- multi-party approval
- supply- chain security assessment

Threat:
- attacker impersonates a legitimate developer using stolen registry credentials and inserts malicious versions of the container images for example inserting backdoors
- malicious registry mirrors impersonate trusted registries and provides compromised images
- DNS poisoning attack redirecting to a fake registry endpoint
- signing service spoofing
Impact:
- unauthorized image download, potentially propritary images revealing important information about the handling of the components of the smart grid and vulnerabilities increasing the probability of completing successful attacks
- upload of compromised images containing backdoors and if installed, giving the attacker the possibility to enter the smart grid in the components using those images,or sending data to unauthorized parties
- authorized credential theft
- supply chain compromise: the malicious images could be deployed for EMS, PDC and SCADA services disrupting their critical functions
- once the malicious changes have been applied they can be used for performing lateral movements, e.g from high-level PDC data analysys to high-level PDC monitoring system
Mitigation:
- strong identity protection with MFA, SSO, PAM and short-lived credentials
- mTLS between registry and client to for ensuring mutual authenticationo
- registry validation, certificate validation and repository pinning
- image signing, trusted signing roots and precise signature verification
- protected discovery service 
- DNSSEC

Threat:
- modification of images stored in the container, inserting backdoors or changing manifests and metadata
- manipulation of the vulnerability scan service to allow the replacement of trusted images with  malicous ones
- change of the state of approval of images from malicious to verified
Impact:
- deployment of compromised workload, mining the stability of the whole architecture because it could have different kind of vulnerabilities for example disclosing sensitive information or opening backdoors
- compromising the vulnerability check service means exposing the system to a high-level of risk potentially introducing and not detecting important issues
- potential compromisation of the whole system using compromised images or even just one of them
Mitigation:
- image integrity: signed images, digest-based deplyment
- SBOM generation and provenance attestation
- indipendent vulnerability scanning and continuous process
- multiple approval for critical changes
- backups, integrity monitoring and audit trails

Threat:
- no proof that a deployer pushed or modified a certain image
- no proof that the admin has introduced or changed something in configuration or access management
- CI/CD operating under a shared identity, so there is no proof of who did what
- unreliable or compromised logs
Impact:
- no accountability for compromised or vulnerable images
- no accountability for configuration changes that may remain hidden and let an attacker compromise the critical services
- delayed incident response extending the exposure of critical components
- compliance issues
Mitigation:
- immutable and centralized logging system with append-only technology
- individual accounts not shared 
- signed commits and images
- admin session monitoring
- trusted time synchonization system

Threat:
- unatuthorized access to private images
- leakage of secrets contained in images
- exposure of vulnerabilities or discovery them by analyzing the code
- disclosure of operational solutions applied based on system weaknesses
- exposure of images metadata that can reveal grid structure or code orgnaization or core idea
Impact:
- disclosure of information that could be usefult for an attacker to have a good understanding of the system, such as configuration, architecture, weaknesses or operational solutions, making easier a successful attack on the grid
- exposure of configuration code that can be used for reverse engineering processes
- loss of compliace for sensitive data exposure
- credential compromise can lead to the attack or compromisation of other components, causing lateral movements to critical services such as SCADA conrtol
Mitigation:
- RBAC and least privilege principle
- secret mangement in external vaults
- secret scanning and avoidance of embedded secrets
- restict repository visibility
- access auditing and DLP mechanisms

Threat:
- flooding the API registry with requests
- exhausting memory by uploading too many images which are useless
- database corruption because of a malware,  vulnerability exploitation or physical failure
- garbage collection misuse
- dependency service failure e.g. authentication, database, object storage
Impact:
- images cannot be uploaded or downloaded, so new application or update to existing ones cannot be provided
- CI/CD pipeline not working, blocking patching of vulnerabilities and updates of systems, making it harder to recover from failures or successful attacks, in a smart grid environment the attacker could exploit the vulnerability for more time without being blocked
- delayed analytics, degraded visibility and monitoring and consequently increased operational risk
Mitigation:
- high availability consisting in replicated registies with geo-redundancy and high availability databases
- API rate limiting and resource quotas
- offline backups
- local caching 
- related system high-availability and resource protection

Threat:
- exploit of misconfigured registry permission or compromisation of the CI/CD admin priviledges
- exploitation of software vulnerabiltiies in the architecture handling the registry
- weak security policies for signing entities or credentials not properly secured
Impact:
- full control of the image repo, ability to publish and also delete trusted images, ability to change the permisions and make the files public
- potential disclosure of important information, availability and also integrity of the database, potential impact on the smart grid operational plane if this is not properly discovered by the actors.
Mitigation:
- properly configured RBAC policies with strong and proper role separation, deny-by-default
- MFA, SSO and PAM
- dedicated service accounts and workload identities 
- strong separation between development, staging and production
- registry hardeining
- proper vulnerability patches
- zero-trust communication

Threat:
- Attacker uses stolen or forged cloud credentials to access storage
- Compromised service account impersonating a legitimate EMS or high-level PDC to read and write data
- Corss-account trust misconfiguration enabling impersonation
- API key leakage from CI/CD repo or other sources
Impact:
- unauthorized access to data stored, telemetry, results of smart grid analysis, telemetry data, events happened, hisotry of the system, topology of the network, configuration. This data can help in understanding the vulnerability of the grid
- unauthorized upload and download of data, able to change the history of the grid
- compliance violation
- lateral movement into other cloud environment
Mitigation:
- MFA for administrators
- short-lived credentials
- workload identity with proper token rotation
- fine-grained IAM roles and separation of service identities
- mutual authentication for data communication (e.g. mTLS)
- resticted trust relationships with explicit role assumption

Threat:
- alteration / deletion / creation of stored objects about grid history, telemetry data, events, topology of the grid, metadata
- alteration of storage policies
Impact: - corruption of data, impacting further statistics and measurements computed on the grid, potential to impact dependent applications such as control of the grid levels and stability based on old parameters                                                               - alterating the storage policies, the data could be disclosed or lost unintentionally, causing compliance problems and facilitating attackers
Mitigation:
- immutable backups
- object lock
- versioning 
- checksums, cryptographic hashes for data integrity
- encryption 
- signing for critical datasets
- offline backu copies

Threat:
- user or service denies modifying, inserting or deleting data 
- admin denies modifying storage policies and configuration
 - missing or altered audit trails upon malicious activities 
 - use of shared service accounts obsuring accountability
Impact:
- compromised non reputation and accountability
- inability to attribute actions leading to further problems in solving problematic situations in the grid and avoid its repeatability
- slower incident response because of the lost trustworthiness of logs
- compliance violation 
- inability to understand whether an attacker accessed the data and disclosed them
Mitigation:
- centralized and immutable logging system
- individual accounts only
- cryptographic protected logs e.g. signed chains or hash chains
- authenticated time source
- session recording for admin accounts

Threat:
- exposure and download of confidential data about the grid because of access control misconfiguration
 - metadata disclosure revealing system architecture and business operations
Impact:
- Specific data about the smart grid could help the attacker in planning a complete attack on the smart grid having a detailed info about the system. The grid is not directly impacted by this, but there can be problems with regulations compliace
-  Privacy violations in case the data are sensitive about the customers and not correctly protected 
- Increased reconnaisance capabilities
- disclosure of critical datasts for the EMS and monitoring systems can give the attacker information about the data used in the critical service and so help them in compromise the algorithms with FDI
- disclosure of historical data about point of failure revealing the weknesses of the system
Mitigation:
- encryption of data at rest and in transit, with mutual authentication to prevent impersonation
- private endpoints
- monitoring and alerting
- key rotation and strong authentication policies for users

Threat:
- API flooding from authorized or unauthorized users
- exhaustion of storage quotas by introducing fake or useless data
- ransomware attack
- compromise of replication or backup process
Impact:
- storage blocked, potential loss of information that should be saved while the storage is out of order
- potential increasing inaccuracy of statistics calculated based on the data related to the grid
- the system is not totally impacted, but it continuos in its critical functions
Mitigation:
- high availability with multi-region replication and availability zones
- disaster recovery procedures
- rate limiting, quotas and alerting
- capacity monitoring and latency monitoring
- randomware protection with immutable snapshots, versioning and object locks

Threat:
- abuse of permissive roles
- misconfigured access policies
- compromise of cloud admin account
- priviledge escalation for cloud permissions
Impact: - full control of storage resources, possibility to delete it, removing all the data, alter security controls, compromising accounts, controlling storage resources. The storage can be lost while the rest of the system continuous working.
Mitigation:
- fine grained IAM with least privilege principle and deny by default
- strong separation of duties between storage admin, backups operator and application owner
- strong and tested authentication mechanisms 
- continuous permission analysis
- monitoring to detect privilege changes 
- automatic compliance checks

Threat:
- By spoofing credentials or API keys, manifests are introduced as legitimate while they are not. Also inserting these manifests in the backup service would lead to this result if the backup is executed.
- Development identities impersonate production deployment identities
Impact: Unatuthorized infrastructure definitions are trusted, malicious manifests are executed. Operational services such as monitoring EMS or high-level PDC analysis are completely compromised
Mitigation:
- strong identity management with MFA, SSO and PAM
- workload identity with dedicated GitOps, short-lived tokens and identity federation
- signed artifacts, in particular commits and tags
- repository protection with protected banches, trusted mirrors and repository pinning
- separate credentials for the different roles

Threat:
- changes of existing configurations manipultating for example security rules (firewalls, network segmentation, permissione, storage) 
- substitution of configuration with malicious manifests 
- manipulation of Terraform state files causes incorrect resource management
Impact: the attacker reduces the security of the machines in the system, he can also open backdoors and expand the attack surface taking the control of the system. this could affect the state-estimation supporting services or disrupt PMU data aggregation reducing operator situational awareness
Mitigation:
- single source of truth and GitOps automated reconciliation
- sined configurations, in partocular commits and helm charts
- mandarory reviews and approval
- policy as a code with tools such as OPA Gatekeeper
- encrypted terraform state and versioned state files
- drift detection

Threat:
- inability to determine who did changes 
- missing version control
- no audit trails or signed commits
- shared identities among multiple actors using common credentials
- unsined changes, so that the commits cannot be attributed
- missing version history because changes are not tracked
Impact:
- no accountability
- harder to discover attacks and also prevent new ones
- recovery becomes slower
- difficulty in resoring trusted configuration because of the difficulty to restore the last good-state, this leaves the services vulnerable for a bigger period of time
Mitigation:
- version control with Git repositories and immutable history
- centralized logging system ensuring immutability
- no shared accounts
- time synchronization for logs and signing, coming from a trusted source

Threat:
- unauthorized access to manifests repositories
- exposure of configuration of deployment and architecure information
- Leakage of embedded secrets, network topology and service relationships
- tags, naming conventions, labelr and general metadata disclosure
- backup repository made public
Impact:
- attacker discovers important information about the system that could be used for exploiting other vulnerabilities, eg. knowning EMS supporting infrastructure it is possible to target it to disrupt the general state estimation service for the whole grid
- exposed secrets could grant access to other parts of the system, thus increasing the blast radius of the attack
Mitigation:
- strong RBAC policies with proper role separation and least privilege principle
- secret references instead of real secrets exposure
- encryption of configurations at rest and mutual TLS for its communication to other entities
